items.question_text: FAA Order JO 7110.65 prescribes two distinct elements: procedures and phraseology. Why must the order include both rather than prescribing only controller actions?
items.explanation: Per 1-1-1, the order prescribes both procedures and phraseology. Standardisation of actions alone is insufficient — standardised communication is equally critical for preventing misunderstanding between controllers and pilots. Procedures govern what controllers do; phraseology governs how they say it.

choices.text: Standardised actions alone ensure safety; phraseology is included only as a training convenience.
Procedures govern controller actions and phraseology governs standardised communication; both are independently necessary for safe, coordinated operations.
Phraseology is the only binding element; procedures are advisory suggestions that facilities may modify locally.
The order prescribes phraseology only for international operations; domestic controllers use locally developed procedures.

items.question_text: A facility faces a situation where the standard separation minima in FAA Order JO 7110.65 are unsuitable. A supervisor considers documenting reduced minima in a Letter of Agreement (LOA) between two civilian facilities versus pursuing a waiver. Which mechanism may authorise a reduction below the order's minima for civilian operations, and why?
items.explanation: Per 1-1-10, LOAs only supplement the order and may not specify lower minima except when military authority authorises reduced separation between military aircraft. Per 1-1-11, waivers address exceptional or unusual requirements that dictate procedural deviations. For civilian facilities needing minima below the order, a waiver is the correct mechanism — an LOA between civilian facilities cannot lower the order's minima.

choices.text: Either an LOA or a waiver works equally — the facility may choose whichever is faster to process.
An LOA may lower minima for civilian operations if both facilities sign it; a waiver is needed only for military involvement.
An LOA between civilian facilities cannot lower minima below the order; a waiver under the exceptional-or-unusual standard is the correct mechanism for civilian procedural deviations.
Neither an LOA nor a waiver can change minima — only a full revision of the order through JO 7000.5 can alter separation standards.

items.question_text: FAA Order JO 7110.65 describes two distribution mechanisms. What is the key distinction between the mandatory distribution list in subparagraph (a) and the electronic subscription mechanism in subparagraph (b)?
items.explanation: Per 1-1-14, subparagraph (a) lists offices and facilities to which the order IS distributed — a mandatory push to named recipients. Subparagraph (b) places an affirmative duty on organisations to subscribe, view, and download changes — the burden is on the recipient to take action. The two mechanisms are complementary but create different types of obligation.

choices.text: Both are automatic — the order arrives without any action required by the recipient in either case.
Subparagraph (a) lists mandatory distribution recipients; subparagraph (b) places an affirmative duty on organisations to subscribe, view, and download changes.
Subparagraph (a) applies only to military facilities; subparagraph (b) applies only to civilian facilities.
Neither mechanism creates a binding obligation — distribution is informational only.

items.question_text: A trainee compares the Explanation of Changes pages against the marked changes in the order text and finds that the EOC pages include only a subset of the changed text — formatting corrections and punctuation fixes are absent. What principle explains which changes appear on the EOC pages?
items.explanation: Per 1-1-5, the Explanation of Changes pages identify SIGNIFICANT changes. Minor formatting corrections, punctuation fixes, and typographical repairs are not significant changes and are therefore excluded from the EOC pages. The EOC pages provide a summary of substantive revisions, not an exhaustive catalogue of every textual change.

choices.text: The EOC pages should list every change, including formatting; the omissions are errors that must be reported to AJV-P.
The EOC pages identify only significant changes; minor formatting and punctuation corrections are not significant and are therefore excluded.
Formatting changes are never made to the order; any such differences are printing errors.
The EOC pages only list changes that affect separation minima; all other changes are documented in JO 7210.3.

items.question_text: A controller reads that a facility 'should' issue a particular advisory and another paragraph stating the controller 'may' issue a different advisory. What is the operational difference between these two instructions under FAA Order JO 7110.65 word meanings?
items.explanation: Per 1-2-1c and 1-2-1d, 'should' means a procedure is recommended — the controller is expected to do it absent a good reason not to. 'May' means a procedure is optional — the controller decides whether circumstances warrant it. A recommended action carries an expectation; an optional one does not.

choices.text: Both words mean the same thing — the controller may choose whether to comply.
'Should' means recommended (expected absent good reason); 'may' means optional (controller decides based on circumstances).
'Should' means mandatory; 'may' means recommended.
'Should' means the procedure applies only to terminal facilities; 'may' applies to all facilities.

items.question_text: In FAA Order JO 7110.65, how does the function of a note differ from the function of a reference?
items.explanation: Per 1-2-3, notes provide explanatory or factual context relating to directive material. Per 1-2-4, references direct attention to an additional or supporting source of information such as another FAA order, NWS directive, CFR, or Advisory Circular. Notes explain; references point to other sources.

choices.text: Notes point to external sources; references provide explanatory context for directive material.
Notes and references serve the same function and the terms are used interchangeably.
Notes are mandatory; references are optional.
Notes provide explanatory or factual context; references direct attention to additional or supporting sources of information.

items.question_text: In TBL 1-2-1, the abbreviation AAR has two listed meanings: Adapted Arrival Route and Airport Arrival Rate. How does a controller determine which meaning applies when reading FAA Order JO 7110.65?
items.explanation: Per TBL 1-2-1, several abbreviations have multiple listed meanings. Context determines which applies: AAR as Airport Arrival Rate appears in traffic management discussions with numeric rates; AAR as Adapted Arrival Route appears in route descriptions. The controller must use operational context, not list order, to disambiguate.

choices.text: Use the first meaning listed in TBL 1-2-1, as it is the primary definition.
Determine the correct meaning from the operational context in which the abbreviation is used.
All listed meanings apply simultaneously — the controller must satisfy every possible interpretation.
Use the longer meaning for en route operations and the shorter meaning for terminal operations.

items.question_text: Arrange the three course classifications defined in FAA Order JO 7110.65 in order from the smallest angular difference to the largest: (A) Opposite/reciprocal courses, (B) Same courses, (C) Crossing courses.
items.explanation: Per 1-2-2, same courses have angular difference less than 45 degrees (smallest range); crossing courses cover 45 through 135 degrees inclusive (middle range); opposite/reciprocal courses cover greater than 135 through 180 degrees inclusive (largest range). The correct ascending order is B, C, A.

choices.text: B (Same), C (Crossing), A (Opposite/reciprocal).
C (Crossing), B (Same), A (Opposite/reciprocal).
B (Same), A (Opposite/reciprocal), C (Crossing).
A (Opposite/reciprocal), C (Crossing), B (Same).

items.question_text: How does the initial routing of a procedural change recommendation from FAA facility personnel differ from a recommendation submitted by an industry group?
items.explanation: Per 1-1-8a and 1-1-8b, FAA personnel submit recommended changes to facility management first. Recommendations from other sources — including industry groups — should be submitted through appropriate FAA, military, or industry/user channels, not through facility management. The destination (AJV-P) is the same; the initial routing differs by source type.

choices.text: Both go directly to AJV-P without any intermediate routing.
FAA personnel submit to facility management first; industry groups submit through appropriate FAA, military, or industry/user channels.
FAA personnel submit directly to AJV-P; industry groups must route through facility management.
Both must be submitted through the Federal Register public comment process.

items.question_text: What does the annotation WAKE TURBULENCE APPLICATION mean when it appears within a paragraph of FAA Order JO 7110.65?
items.explanation: Per 1-2-5f, WAKE TURBULENCE APPLICATION inserted within a paragraph means that the remaining information in the paragraph requires the application of wake turbulence procedures. The annotation does not label a separate wake turbulence section — it marks that wake turbulence rules apply to the remainder of the paragraph in which the annotation appears.

choices.text: Only the sentence immediately preceding the annotation requires wake turbulence procedures.
The paragraph is an advisory discussion of wake turbulence and creates no mandatory procedures.
Wake turbulence procedures must be applied to all information that follows the annotation within that section of the order.
Wake turbulence separation is waived for all operations described in that paragraph.

items.question_text: What do the change number and effective date on a revised page of FAA Order JO 7110.65 tell the controller, and are they printed on every page?
items.explanation: Per 1-2-5a, the change number and effective date are printed on each revised or additional page — not on every page of the order. They allow the controller to verify at a glance that the page reflects the current change level. An unrevised page that does not require a change is reprinted in its original form per 1-2-5b.

choices.text: They are printed on every page of the order so controllers can verify currency throughout.
They are printed on each revised or additional page only, allowing quick verification that the page is at the current change level.
They appear only on the Explanation of Changes pages, not on the procedure pages themselves.
They are printed on the cover page only; internal pages use a separate date-tracking system.

items.question_text: FAA Order JO 7110.65 applies to two categories of persons. Which statement correctly identifies both categories?
items.explanation: Per 1-1-2, the order applies to all ATO personnel AND anyone using ATO directives. The first category covers employees within the Air Traffic Organization regardless of role. The second category captures contractors, military personnel, and others who use ATO-published procedures — regardless of employment relationship.

choices.text: Only FAA-certified air traffic controllers and facility managers.
All ATO personnel and anyone using ATO directives.
Only FAA employees and military personnel assigned to joint-use facilities.
Only persons holding a current FAA medical certificate and security clearance.

items.question_text: Under FAA Order JO 7110.65, what rule determines which FAA region a facility must contact when the order references a non-ATO regional office organization?
items.explanation: Per 1-1-13, the facility contacts the FAA region where the facility is physically located — not the region where its service area office is located. Physical geography governs over organisational alignment for non-ATO regional contacts. The examples given (Communications Center, Flight Standards, Airport offices) are organisations outside the Air Traffic Organization.

choices.text: Contact the region where the facility's service area office is located.
Contact the region where the facility is physically located.
Contact whichever region most recently issued a directive on the subject matter.
Contact Washington headquarters directly in all cases.

items.instruction: For each situation, decide whether the controller must follow a prescribed procedure or may exercise best judgment.
items.question_text: In which of the following situations must the controller exercise best judgment rather than apply a specific published procedure?

choices.text: An aircraft requests an approach to an airport served by a published instrument approach procedure that the controller regularly uses.
An unusual mix of VFR and IFR traffic creates a conflict scenario that is not addressed by any provision in the order, and the controller must resolve it safely.
Two aircraft on the same airway at the same altitude are closing, and the order prescribes a specific separation minimum and vectoring procedure.
A controller receives a handoff using the standard coordination procedure described in the order and must decide whether to accept it.

items.explanation: Per 1-1-1, controllers must exercise best judgment when they encounter situations NOT covered by the order. When a published procedure applies, the controller follows it. Best judgment is the fallback for uncovered situations, not a discretionary alternative to existing procedures.

items.instruction: For each situation, determine whether a Letter of Agreement (LOA) or a Waiver is the appropriate mechanism.
items.question_text: In which of the following situations is a waiver the appropriate mechanism rather than an LOA?

choices.text: Two civilian TRACONs want to document joint handoff procedures that operate within existing order minima and require mutual coordination.
A civilian facility has an exceptional operational requirement that dictates a procedural deviation below the order's separation minima, and no military operations are involved.
An ARTCC and an adjacent approach control want to formalise standard coordination procedures that both facilities already follow in practice.
Two facilities need to document agreed-upon altitude assignments for arriving traffic that stay within the order's prescribed ranges.

items.explanation: Per 1-1-10, LOAs document jointly applied procedures or minima requiring multi-facility cooperation — but their minima may not fall below the order's except for military-authorised reduced separation between military aircraft. Per 1-1-11, waivers address exceptional or unusual requirements that dictate procedural deviations — this is the mechanism for a civilian facility needing a deviation below the order's minima.

items.question_text: Which list correctly identifies all three channels that FAA Order JO 7110.65 specifies for obtaining additional ATO SMS information?

choices.text: SMS Toolbox, emailing AJI at 9-AJI-SMS@faa.gov, and contacting the service center Quality Control Group.
SMS Toolbox, facility quality assurance program, and FAA Hotline.
Emailing AJI, filing a UCR, and contacting the regional administrator.
MyFAA portal, eLMS training module, and the service center Quality Control Group.

items.explanation: Per 1-1-12, three channels are identified for obtaining additional SMS information: visiting the SMS Toolbox, emailing AJI at 9-AJI-SMS@faa.gov, and contacting the service center Quality Control Group. Other FAA channels serve different purposes and are not listed as SMS information channels in this paragraph.

items.instruction: Choose the correct notification target for each facility type.
items.question_text: When the order or changes have not been received at least 30 days before the effective date, to whom must an FAA facility report non-receipt, and to whom must a military facility report?

choices.text: An FAA facility notifies its service area office distribution officer; a military facility notifies its appropriate military headquarters.
Both FAA and military facilities notify AJV-P directly.
An FAA facility notifies its military headquarters; a military facility notifies its service area office distribution officer.
Both facility types notify the GovDelivery subscription administrator.

items.explanation: Per 1-1-7b, FAA facilities notify their service area office distribution officer. Per 1-1-7c, military facilities notify their appropriate military headquarters. The notification targets are distinct and based on facility type.

lookup_context: A state aircraft commander plans to operate under due regard
The pilot must meet two obligations and satisfy at least one of four operational conditions
items.task: Verify that a pilot's stated operational plan satisfies the due regard conditions by checking against the complete list in the order.
items.instruction: Use FAA Order JO 7110.65 paragraph 1-2-1o to verify the pilot's plan.
items.question_text: A state aircraft commander states the following plan: they will separate from all other air traffic, have arranged with a military unit for search and rescue monitoring, and will operate in Class G airspace. Has the commander satisfied the minimum requirements for due regard operations?

choices.text: Yes; all requirements are met — both obligations are satisfied plus at least one of the four operational conditions.
No; the commander must also be in communication with ATC even in Class G airspace.
No; the commander must satisfy at least two of the four operational conditions.
No; Class G airspace is not one of the acceptable operational conditions for due regard.

items.explanation: Per 1-2-1o, due regard requires: obligation (a) separate from all other air traffic; obligation (b) assure SAR monitoring; and at least one condition from (c): VMC, or ATC surveillance + communications, or airborne radar, or Class G airspace. The commander's plan satisfies both obligations and meets condition (c)(4) — Class G airspace — so all minimum requirements are met.

lookup_context: Course definitions require classification into same, crossing, or opposite/reciprocal based on angular difference
FIG 1-2-1 depicts the angular divergence bands visually
items.task: Use the course-definition figure and the associated paragraph to classify a course relationship by angular difference.
items.instruction: Use FIG 1-2-1 and the course definitions in FAA Order JO 7110.65.
items.question_text: Using the course definitions and FIG 1-2-1, classify two courses whose protected airspaces intersect with an angular difference of 140 degrees.

choices.text: Opposite/reciprocal courses, because the angular difference is greater than 135 degrees and the protected airspaces intersect.
Crossing courses, because the angular difference is between 45 and 180 degrees.
Same courses, because the angular difference is less than 180 degrees.
The classification is undefined because protected airspaces intersect rather than overlap.

items.explanation: Per 1-2-2c, opposite/reciprocal courses are courses whose protected airspaces are coincident, overlap, or intersect AND whose angular difference is greater than 135 degrees through 180 degrees inclusive. At 140 degrees with intersecting protected airspaces, both conditions are satisfied — this is opposite/reciprocal.

items.situation: A controller is applying a separation procedure in FAA Order JO 7110.65 and sees a reference block citing another FAA order. The controller assumes the referenced order must be consulted and followed before any action can be taken.
items.para_context: A reference directs attention to additional or supporting information; it does not independently add a mandatory procedural step.
items.question_text: Does a reference block in FAA Order JO 7110.65 create an additional mandatory procedure that the controller must follow before acting?

choices.text: Yes; all references create binding obligations that must be satisfied before the referencing procedure can be applied.
Yes, but only if the reference cites another chapter within JO 7110.65 itself.
No; references direct attention to supporting information but do not, by themselves, create additional mandatory procedural steps.
No; references are decorative elements that have no operational function.

items.explanation: Per 1-2-4, references direct attention to an additional or supporting source of information. They alert the reader to where related or supporting material can be found, but they do not independently create new mandatory procedures. The controller should be aware of the referenced material but need not treat every reference as a prerequisite.

items.instruction: Evaluate whether the controller's action is consistent with the order's guidance on rephrasing prescribed phraseology.
items.question_text: A controller issues a landing clearance using the prescribed phraseology, then rephrases the message with different words after the pilot sounds uncertain. Is this permitted under FAA Order JO 7110.65?

choices.text: Yes; controllers may rephrase after first using the prescribed phraseology, provided they exercise good judgment and ensure the content is understood.
No; once prescribed phraseology is used, no further transmission on the same subject is permitted.
Yes; controllers may rephrase at any time and need not use prescribed phraseology first.
No; rephrasing is only permitted when the annotation EXAMPLE appears in the paragraph.

items.explanation: Per the note following 1-2-5g, controllers may, after first using the prescribed phraseology for a specific procedure, rephrase the message to ensure the content is understood. Good judgment must be exercised. The prescribed words come first; rephrasing is permitted as a follow-up comprehension aid.

items.situation: A controller reading a traffic management directive sees the abbreviation AAR used in the sentence: 'The AAR for ATL is currently 32.' A trainee claims AAR always means Adapted Arrival Route. The controller needs to determine the correct meaning.
items.para_context: When an abbreviation has more than one approved meaning, determine the intended meaning from its operational context.
items.question_text: In the sentence 'The AAR for ATL is currently 32,' what does AAR most likely mean, and how should the controller make this determination?

choices.text: Adapted Arrival Route, because AAR always defaults to the first definition listed in TBL 1-2-1.
Airport Arrival Rate, because the numeric value (32) in context of traffic management indicates a flow rate, not a route.
The controller must use both meanings simultaneously — 32 Adapted Arrival Routes at a rate.
Adverse Assumption Obstacle, because traffic management directives use safety abbreviations.

items.explanation: Per TBL 1-2-1, AAR has two listed meanings: Adapted Arrival Route and Airport Arrival Rate. Context determines which applies. A numeric value ('32') used with an airport identifier (ATL) in a traffic management context indicates a flow rate — Airport Arrival Rate.

items.instruction: For each person, determine whether FAA Order JO 7110.65 applies to them.
items.question_text: To which of the following persons does FAA Order JO 7110.65 apply?

choices.text: A military contractor using ATO-published separation standards while developing procedures for a DoD range operation.
A civilian software engineer developing a flight-planning tool who has never read or used any ATO directive.
An airline dispatcher who does not use FAA ATC directives in their work.
A private pilot who flies recreationally and does not use ATO directives.

items.explanation: Per 1-1-2, the order applies to all ATO personnel AND anyone using ATO directives. The military contractor using ATO separation standards falls under 'anyone using ATO directives.' The other persons listed do not use ATO directives and are not ATO personnel, so the order does not apply to them.

items.front: What two distinct elements does FAA Order JO 7110.65 prescribe for persons providing ATC services?
items.back: Air traffic control procedures and phraseology. Procedures govern what controllers do; phraseology governs what controllers say. Both are required for standardised, safe operations.

items.front: What two conditions must both be present before an LOA can specify separation minima lower than those in FAA Order JO 7110.65?
items.back: 1) Appropriate military authority must have authorised it, AND 2) the reduced separation must be between military aircraft. Both conditions must be met simultaneously. Civilian facilities may never lower the order's minima through an LOA.

items.front: When contacting a non-ATO FAA regional office, which FAA region should a facility use — the region of its physical location or its service area office?
items.back: The region where the facility is physically located — not the region where its service area office is located. Physical geography governs over organisational alignment for non-ATO regional contacts.

items.front: Where is the text of FAA Order JO 7110.65 available online, and how does this differ from where published interpretations are available?
items.back: The order text is available on two public FAA websites: Air Traffic Plans and Publications, and Orders and Notices. Published interpretations are available on the MyFAA employee portal (an internal system). The order is public; interpretations are internal.

items.front: How does the initial submission path for a procedural change recommendation differ between FAA facility personnel and an external industry source?
items.back: FAA personnel submit recommended changes to facility management first. Recommendations from other sources (industry, military, users) go through appropriate FAA, military, or industry/user channels — not through facility management. The destination (AJV-P) is the same; the initial routing differs by source type.

items.front: At exactly 135 degrees angular difference with intersecting protected airspaces, are two aircraft on crossing courses or opposite/reciprocal courses?
items.back: Crossing courses. Crossing includes 45 through 135 degrees inclusive. Opposite/reciprocal starts above 135 degrees (greater than 135). The 135-degree boundary falls within crossing, not opposite/reciprocal.

items.front: What is the function of a note in FAA Order JO 7110.65, and does a note create independent mandatory requirements?
items.back: A note sets forth statements of fact or prefatory/explanatory material relating to directive material. Notes explain or provide context — they do not create independent mandatory requirements. The directive text governs; notes support understanding of it.
